title: Avocado and green clay cold-process soap
subtitle: A conditioning olive-and-avocado bar with French green clay
excerpt: A gentle cold-process bar built around avocado oil and shea butter for conditioning, with French green clay for a soft mineral colour and mild drawing action. The formula produces a creamy, long-lasting bar suited to dry or sensitive skin.
sourceNotes: SAP values used: avocado oil 0.133, coconut oil 0.190, shea butter 0.128, castor oil 0.128. 5% superfat applied; water as 33% of oil weight. Reference: Susan Miller Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997), chapters on conditioning oils and oil-property tables. USDA AOCS fatty acid database, avocado oil oleic and palmitoleic profile. French green clay properties: Rose-Marie Nichols McGee and Maggie Stuckey, The Herb Companion, sourced via open botanical references.

recipe.batchNotes: Run any change to oil weights through a lye calculator before scaling. Adjust the clay addition proportionally.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Allow 2 days in the mould before unmoulding, then at least 4 weeks on a curing rack. Six weeks gives a harder, milder bar.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Store cured bars on an open rack in a cool, dark cupboard. Fully cured bars keep two years. Discard if orange oily spots (DOS) appear or the bar develops a sharp paint-like smell, both signs of rancid superfat.

glossaryTerms.definition: The chemical reaction between fat and sodium hydroxide that produces soap and glycerine. Each oil has a SAP value: the grams of lye needed to fully saponify one gram of that oil.
The percentage of fat left unsaponified in the finished bar, typically 5 to 8 percent. At 5% superfat the bar is mild and conditioning with a small reserve of free oil.
The point at which soap batter thickens enough that a drizzle on the surface briefly holds its shape. The signal that oils and lye solution have emulsified and it is safe to pour.

projectSchedule.title: Unmould and cut
projectSchedule.body: Turn out the loaf. Avocado-oil bars are soft; if the soap feels sticky, leave it another day before cutting. Wear protective gloves: the bar is still caustic at this stage.

projectSchedule.title: Turn bars
projectSchedule.body: Flip each bar so the underside hardens evenly. The clay lends the bar a pale sage-green tone that deepens slightly as moisture leaves.

projectSchedule.title: Four-week check
projectSchedule.body: Test a sliver on the inside of the forearm. If there is no sting, the bar is ready to use from this point.

projectSchedule.title: Cure complete
projectSchedule.body: A fully cured bar is firm, pale green, and noticeably mild. Store on an open rack in a cool dark place.

content.text: This bar is built for conditioning. Avocado oil contributes palmitoleic acid, which is close in structure to the fatty acids in skin sebum, making it particularly kind to dry or sensitive skin. Shea butter adds a creamy lather and long-lasting moisturising quality. The coconut oil provides cleansing and bubbly lather; the castor oil stabilises the foam. French green clay gives the bar a soft mineral colour and a mild drawing quality without altering the lather. At 5%
superfat
and a 33% lye discount, the finished bar is gentle, hard, and long-lasting. Cure time is 4 to 6 weeks. Yield is 7 bars of around 130 g each.
Before starting, put on protective gloves and goggles. Lye handling comes before anything else.
Ingredients

items.prepNote: 30% of oil weight; melt gently if solid
NaOH at 99% purity; calculated for 5% superfat on this oil blend
3% of oil weight; adds colour and a mild drawing quality

content.text: Method
Mix the lye solution

Work in a ventilated space. Put on protective gloves and goggles before opening the lye container. Weigh the sodium hydroxide into a small container. Pour the distilled water into the HDPE lye pitcher. Add the lye to the water in a steady stream, stirring with a heatproof spoon. Never add water to lye. The solution heats sharply and gives off fumes; stand back and stir steadily. Set the pitcher somewhere safe to cool to 38 to 42°C.
Prepare the oils

Weigh the coconut oil and shea butter into a heatproof bowl. Melt gently over a low heat until just liquid. Remove from the heat and add the avocado oil and castor oil. Stir to combine. Let the oil blend cool to 38 to 42°C.
Add the clay and blend to trace

Disperse the French green clay in a small amount of the oil blend (about 2 tablespoons) to form a smooth paste. This prevents clay lumping in the final batter. Pour the lye solution into the oil blend in a slow stream. Use the stick blender in short 5-second bursts, stirring between each, until the batter reaches light
trace
: the consistency of thin custard. Stir in the clay paste and blend briefly until fully incorporated. The batter will turn a soft pale green.
Pour and insulate
Pour the batter into the silicone loaf mould. Tap the mould once to settle. Wrap in a towel and leave undisturbed for 24 to 48 hours. The heat of
saponification

will drive the soap through a gel phase if it gets warm enough. An avocado-oil bar may show a translucent centre zone after cutting; this is normal and fades as the bar matures over the following weeks.
Unmould, cut, and cure

After 2 days, turn out the loaf. High-avocado bars are softer than tallow or coconut bars; if the loaf still feels sticky, leave it another 24 hours. Cut into bars wearing protective gloves. Arrange on a rack with space between each bar and leave in a cool, dry room out of direct sunlight for 4 to 6 weeks. Turn each bar every two weeks. The pale green tone deepens slightly as the bar dries and firms.
Storage and shelf life

Store cured bars on an open soap dish or rack. A closed container traps moisture and shortens shelf life. In a cool dark cupboard, fully cured bars keep two years. Discard any bar showing orange oily spots (DOS, dreaded orange spots) or a sharp paint-like smell. Both indicate rancid superfat oil from the avocado or shea content.
